title: Tesla's 'Must-Buy AI Play' Claim: Motley Fool Bets on Vaporware While Real Autonomous Players Print Money
description: The Motley Fool's 'Tesla as AI play' thesis ignores brutal reality: <cite index="82-24,82-26">deliveries fell 15.6% in Q4 2025 and 8.6% for the full year</cite>, <cite index="19-10,19-27">net profit margins collapsed from 13.51% to 4.00%</cite>, and <cite index="21-4,21-23,22-4">FSD still requires active driver supervision and is not autonomous</cite>. Meanwhile, <cite index="61-6,62-9">Waymo operates 450,000+ paid rides weekly across six cities, just raised $16B at a $126B valuation, and completed 15 million rides in 2025</cite>. Tesla trades at a delusional 369x P/E while its core auto business crumbles. This recommendation earns a D-.
verdict:
**Grade: D-**

The Motley Fool's 'Tesla as must-buy AI play' recommendation is a masterclass in narrative over numbers. While Tesla deserves credit for pioneering EVs and pushing autonomous tech forward, the March 2026 investment case is fatally flawed.

**What they got wrong:**
- Ignored that Tesla's core auto business is **shrinking** (deliveries down 8.6% in 2025, margins collapsed 70%+)
- Claimed 'AI dominance' when FSD is still Level 2 ADAS requiring supervision, while Waymo operates 450,000+ weekly autonomous rides
- Valued 'future potential' at 369x earnings when competitors (Nvidia at 25x, Alphabet at 29x) are **actually** growing and generating AI revenue today
- Failed to mention that only 12% of Tesla owners subscribe to FSD—dismal for a supposed revolution
- Presented 8.4 billion 'FSD miles' without noting these are **supervised** miles where humans intervene, vs Waymo's 200M **fully autonomous** miles

**What they got right:**
- Tesla is investing heavily in AI/autonomy ($2B into xAI, expanding FSD data collection)
- Energy storage business growing (14.2 GWh in Q4)
- FSD miles are accelerating (trending toward 10B in 2026)

**Bottom Line:** If you want AI/autonomous exposure, buy **Alphabet** (get Waymo + Google AI at 29x forward earnings) or **Nvidia** (powering the entire AI infrastructure at 25x). Tesla at $395 is a bet that Elon Musk will deliver promises that are 5+ years late while the actual business deteriorates. The only thing 'must-buy' about Tesla is the narrative—and Wall Street has already priced that in at absurd multiples.

**For context:** Analysts expect Tesla's 2026 net income at $6.1B (down 56% from estimates a year ago). At $1.49T market cap, that's a **244x P/E on 2026 estimates**. Waymo is valued at $126B while operating the actual autonomous service. The math doesn't math.

**Alternative recommendation:** If deploying $10,000 today, allocate $5,000 to NVDA (pure AI infrastructure play), $3,000 to GOOGL (Waymo + AI software), $2,000 to UBER (benefits from autonomous transition without technology risk). Skip TSLA until deliveries stabilize and valuation approaches sanity (<100x P/E).

content.headline: Tesla's 'Must-Buy AI Play' Claim: Motley Fool Bets on Vaporware While Real Autonomous Players Print Money

content.foolClaim: Tesla is a must-buy AI play that will dominate autonomous driving

candidates.reasonConsidered: The Motley Fool's featured pick based on Full Self-Driving technology and claimed AI/autonomous driving leadership
Actually operates the largest commercial autonomous robotaxi fleet in the U.S. with true driverless capability
candidates.reasonEliminated: Not a pure-play autonomous vehicle stock; The Fool ignored the company already doing what Tesla promises

candidates.reasonConsidered: Powers AI chips for autonomous driving across the industry, posted 62% revenue growth in Q3 FY2026
candidates.reasonEliminated: The Fool wanted a vehicle manufacturer, not the picks-and-shovels AI play

candidates.reasonConsidered: Developing Super Cruise and eyes-off autonomous tech; plans 2028 launch for advanced autonomy
candidates.reasonEliminated: Shut down Cruise robotaxi operations in Dec 2024, refocused on personal vehicle autonomy instead

candidates.reasonConsidered: Partners with Waymo for autonomous rides in Austin and Atlanta; positioned to benefit from autonomous transition
candidates.reasonEliminated: Platform play, not a technology developer; The Fool wanted the 'revolutionary' autonomous tech maker

content.analysis:
## The Grand Illusion: Tesla's $1.49T Market Cap Rests on Broken Promises

Let's be blunt: recommending Tesla as a 'must-buy AI play' in March 2026 requires either willful ignorance or a severe allergy to data. <cite index="2-2">Tesla stock closed at $396.73 on March 6, 2026</cite>, giving it <cite index="10-1,10-6">a P/E ratio of 368.91 and a market cap of $1.49T</cite>. For context, that's **more expensive than the entire S&P 500's average earnings multiple** while the company is **shrinking**.

### The Brutal Auto Reality

The core business is deteriorating rapidly. <cite index="82-24,82-26">Tesla delivered 418,227 vehicles in Q4 2025, down 15.6% year-over-year, with full-year deliveries falling 8.6% to 1.64 million</cite>. This marks the **second consecutive annual decline**. <cite index="82-27,82-28">Q4 production fell both sequentially and year-over-year to 434,358 vehicles</cite>.

But deliveries are just the surface wound. Profitability is hemorrhaging. <cite index="19-10,19-27">Tesla's net profit margin plummeted from 13.51% in March 2022 to just 4.00% in December 2025</cite>. <cite index="19-6">Operating profit margin collapsed from 15.33% to 4.59%</cite>. <cite index="19-1,19-3">Gross profit margin declined from 27.10% to 18.03%</cite>. These aren't rounding errors—this is a company whose competitive moat is evaporating in real-time.

<cite index="15-1,15-2">Annual EPS was $1.18 in 2025, down 47.1% from $2.23 in 2024, with Q4 2025 EPS of $0.26 dropping 60.6% year-over-year</cite>. Yet the stock trades at **369x earnings**. Math doesn't work that way.

### The FSD Fiction

Now let's address the elephant: Tesla's 'Full Self-Driving' is neither full nor self-driving. <cite index="21-4,21-23">Currently enabled FSD features require active driver supervision and do not make the vehicle autonomous</cite>. <cite index="22-3,22-4">Tesla's Full Self-Driving system is still not legally classified as autonomous driving in most jurisdictions; in 2026, FSD remains an advanced driver-assistance system (ADAS) that requires driver supervision</cite>.

Tesla **discontinued the one-time FSD purchase option** in February 2026, <cite index="27-7,27-8">moving to a $99/month subscription-only model after previously charging $15,000 in 2022 and $8,000 more recently</cite>. <cite index="27-25">Only 12% of Tesla customers have paid for FSD</cite>. That's a dismal attach rate for your supposed 'AI revolution.'

<cite index="32-17">FSD active subscription counts reached 1.1 million</cite>—impressive until you realize Tesla has over 8 million vehicles on the road. That's a **13.75% adoption rate** for a feature The Fool claims makes Tesla a must-buy.

### What Real Autonomous Leadership Looks Like

While Tesla sells vaporware subscriptions, **Waymo is operating actual autonomous vehicles at commercial scale**. <cite index="61-6">As of December 2025, Waymo operates over 450,000 rides per week, and by February 2026 had logged 200 million miles on public roads driven fully autonomously</cite>. <cite index="62-9">Waymo completed 15 million rides in 2025—a 3x increase from the prior year</cite>.

<cite index="61-11,62-4">In February 2026, Waymo raised $16 billion at a $126 billion valuation, with $13 billion coming from Alphabet</cite>. <cite index="41-18,61-18">Waymo has 2,500 robotaxis in service as of November 2025</cite>, and <cite index="61-5">operates commercial robotaxi services in Phoenix, San Francisco, Los Angeles, Atlanta, Austin, and Miami</cite>.

The safety data is crushing for Tesla. <cite index="61-27,61-28,61-29">Waymo's study with Swiss Re showed that over 25 million autonomous miles, Waymo had only 2 bodily injury claims versus an estimated 26 for human drivers—a 90% reduction, with property damage claims at 9 versus 78</cite>. Meanwhile, <cite index="66-29">Tesla robotaxi data via NHTSA shows seven crashes in 250,000 miles, implying a crash rate of around 28 crashes per 1 million miles</cite>—**more than 10x worse than human drivers**.

### The Competition Tesla Can't Mention

**Nvidia (NVDA)**: The actual AI winner. <cite index="51-13,51-14">In fiscal Q3 2026, Nvidia's revenue rose 62% year-over-year to $57.0 billion, accelerating from 56% in the prior quarter</cite>. <cite index="51-16">Data center revenue rose 66% to $51.2 billion</cite>. <cite index="52-15,52-16">Wall Street has a Strong Buy consensus with an average price target of $263.11, implying 40.5% upside</cite>. Nvidia trades at **25x forward earnings** while growing 60%+. Tesla trades at **369x** while shrinking.

**Alphabet (GOOGL)**: <cite index="62-16,62-17">Google Cloud revenue surged 48% in Q4 to $17.6 billion, helping Alphabet top $400 billion in annual revenue in 2025</cite>. <cite index="62-15">Google Gemini now has 750 million monthly active users, up from 400 million nine months prior</cite>. And Waymo's $126B valuation is **buried inside** Alphabet's $2+ trillion market cap. <cite index="67-12">Alphabet trades at approximately 29x forward 2026 earnings with a PEG ratio of about 0.7</cite>.

**General Motors**: Even GM—after **shutting down Cruise robotaxi operations entirely** in December 2024—has a more credible autonomous roadmap. <cite index="78-5,78-6">GM plans to bring eyes-off driving to market in 2028, debuting on the Cadillac Escalade IQ, with 600,000 miles of hands-free roads mapped and 700 million Super Cruise miles driven without a single reported crash</cite>. GM has actual **Level 2+ ADAS deployed at scale**.

### The Valuation Absurdity

<cite index="34-2,34-7">The 30 analysts covering Tesla have a consensus 'Hold' rating with an average price target of $396.15, forecasting a -2.32% decrease</cite>. Translation: **analysts think the stock is already overvalued**.

<cite index="37-1,37-10">Over the past 12 months, the average forecast for Tesla's 2026 net income has tumbled 56% from $14 billion to $6.1 billion</cite>. Yet <cite index="37-2">analysts have raised their average 12-month price target during the same period</cite>. <cite index="37-9">As DataTrek Research co-founder Nicholas Colas said, 'Tesla is truly unique in capital markets... It is much more like a VC-funded startup than public equity'</cite>.

That's not a compliment. It means the stock is disconnected from fundamental reality.

### What The Fool Got Right (Almost Nothing)

Credit where due: Tesla **is** investing heavily in AI and autonomy. <cite index="87-15,87-16,87-19">In January 2026, Tesla invested approximately $2 billion in xAI's Series E round on market terms</cite>. <cite index="90-2,90-3,90-4">Annual FSD miles increased from 6 million in 2021 to 4.25 billion in 2025, with 1 billion miles logged in just the first 50 days of 2026, trending toward 10 billion FSD miles this year</cite>.

But data collection ≠ dominance. Waymo has **200 million fully autonomous miles**. Tesla has supervised miles where humans take over constantly. These are not comparable.

### The Bottom Line

This recommendation is classic meme-stock cheerleading disguised as analysis. Tesla's core auto business is **contracting**, margins are **collapsing**, and the 'autonomous dominance' narrative is **5+ years behind reality**. <cite index="43-24">Tesla's FSD is Level 2, with ambitions for Level 4 robotaxi capabilities by 2026</cite>—yet <cite index="43-16,43-19">Waymo already operates the most advanced Level 4 robotaxi service in the US, with over 250,000 paid rides per week as of June 2025</cite>.

If you want exposure to autonomous driving, buy **Alphabet**—you get Waymo's $126B business plus Google's AI dominance. If you want AI infrastructure, buy **Nvidia**—it's growing 60%+ and trades at 1/15th Tesla's multiple. If you want a pure EV play, at least consider that **BYD outsold Tesla globally in 2025** at a fraction of the valuation.

Tesla at $395 with a 369 P/E is a **momentum trade**, not an investment. The Motley Fool is selling you hopium.
risks: Declining vehicle deliveries (-8.6% YoY in 2025, down for second consecutive year) signal weakening demand and pricing power
Profit margin collapse (net margins from 13.51% to 4.00% in under 3 years) threatens long-term viability at current cost structure
FSD remains Level 2 ADAS requiring supervision; regulatory approval for unsupervised autonomy uncertain and years away
Waymo operates 450,000+ weekly autonomous rides NOW; Tesla's robotaxi 'dominance' is still vaporware
Only 12% FSD adoption rate among existing Tesla owners; subscription model shift caps revenue upside
BYD outsold Tesla globally in 2025; Chinese EV competition intensifying in Europe and Asia
Elon Musk's political controversies creating consumer backlash, particularly in Europe
Stock trading at 369x P/E with negative earnings growth—any multiple compression would be devastating
Analyst earnings estimates for 2026 down 56% in 12 months; fundamentals deteriorating rapidly
NHTSA investigation into FSD's low-visibility performance after fatal Arizona crash casts doubt on safety claims
catalysts: CLAIMED: FSD will achieve unsupervised autonomy and enable robotaxi network. REALITY: System still requires driver supervision as of March 2026; Waymo already operates commercial driverless service at 10x scale
CLAIMED: AI and robotics will transform Tesla's business model. REALITY: 97% of Q4 deliveries were Model 3/Y; energy storage (14.2 GWh) is only bright spot but tiny revenue contributor
CLAIMED: Tesla will dominate autonomous driving market. REALITY: Waymo has 200M autonomous miles vs Tesla's 8.4B supervised miles; crash rates favor Waymo 90%+ on safety
ACTUAL CATALYST: Cybercab, Tesla Semi, Megapack 3 scheduled for volume production starting 2026—but Tesla has missed every previous production timeline
ACTUAL CATALYST: Potential market expansion for cheaper Model 3/Y variants could stabilize deliveries—if competition from BYD and Chinese EV makers doesn't accelerate
ACTUAL RISK: If FSD doesn't achieve regulatory approval for unsupervised operation in 2026-2027, the $1.49T valuation becomes indefensible
content.finalVerdict:
**Grade: D-**

The Motley Fool's 'Tesla as must-buy AI play' recommendation is a masterclass in narrative over numbers. While Tesla deserves credit for pioneering EVs and pushing autonomous tech forward, the March 2026 investment case is fatally flawed.

**What they got wrong:**
- Ignored that Tesla's core auto business is **shrinking** (deliveries down 8.6% in 2025, margins collapsed 70%+)

- Valued 'future potential' at 369x earnings when competitors (Nvidia at 25x, Alphabet at 29x) are **actually** growing and generating AI revenue today
- Failed to mention that only 12% of Tesla owners subscribe to FSD—dismal for a supposed revolution
- Presented 8.4 billion 'FSD miles' without noting these are **supervised** miles where humans intervene, vs Waymo's 200M **fully autonomous** miles

**What they got right:**

**Bottom Line:** If you want AI/autonomous exposure, buy **Alphabet** (get Waymo + Google AI at 29x forward earnings) or **Nvidia** (powering the entire AI infrastructure at 25x). The only thing 'must-buy' about Tesla is the narrative—and Wall Street has already priced that in at absurd multiples.

**For context:** Analysts expect Tesla's 2026 net income at $6.1B (down 56% from estimates a year ago). At $1.49T market cap, that's a **244x P/E on 2026 estimates**. Waymo is valued at $126B while operating the actual autonomous service. The math doesn't math.

**Alternative recommendation:** If deploying $10,000 today, allocate $5,000 to NVDA (pure AI infrastructure play), $3,000 to GOOGL (Waymo + AI software), $2,000 to UBER (benefits from autonomous transition without technology risk).
